steps.title: Identify what Chen's finding must logically suggest
steps.content: The passage states the standard economic view was that liberalization "would make it easier for companies to raise money from investors." Chen then found that companies in 11 countries received "significant increases in investment in the three years following liberalization" before the text says "suggesting that ______" — so the answer must be the conclusion Chen's data supports. Since his empirical finding matches what economists predicted, the suggestion is that the data confirmed the standard view.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
The data showed investment increased after liberalization — exactly what economists predicted — so the answer must align those two facts.
  • Choice A — empirical evidence was consistent with the scholarly consensus about the consequences of liberalization.: correctly states that Chen's finding (increased investment) matched the economists' prediction (easier to raise money), i.e., the data confirmed the consensus. ✓
  • Choice B — economists who held the standard view of liberalization failed to anticipate some serious negative effects of liberalization.: no exception is described. ✗
  • Choice C — companies did not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurred.: opposite of the passage; Chen found significant investment increases in the three years following liberalization. ✗
  • Choice D — companies in Malaysia experienced a greater increase in investment following liberalization than did companies in the Philippines.: no comparison between Malaysia and the Philippines is provided. ✗
